text: The surgeon localizes the previous ileocolic anastomosis and sections the terminal ileum using a linear stapler. Ileocolic vessels are identified at the previous ligation site, and dissection continues proximally to their origin using both blunt and sharp techniques. The confluence of the ileocolic vein and superior mesenteric vein is visible, where the vein is ligated and cut. The artery, crossing behind the superior mesenteric vein, is also cut. The supracolic phase begins with the laser momentum directed away until the gastroepiploic vein is visible. The laser sac is opened to facilitate colic mobilization. The tissue removed corresponds to the DII lymphadenectomy area. For a DIII lymphadenectomy, dissection follows the superior mesenteric vein upwards. The colic branch of Henle is identified, ligated, and sectioned, while the middle colic vessels are exposed and sectioned, revealing the anatomy of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.

The surgeon completes the DIII lymphadenectomy, revealing the vascular bed with the superior mesenteric vein and its branches, including the gastrocolic trunk of Henle and middle colic vessels. The gastrocolic ligament is dissected up to the splenic angle, which is then freed. The inferior mesenteric vein and left colic vein are sectioned, followed by the identification and sectioning of the inferior mesenteric artery and left colic branch. The descending colon is transected with linear staples, and an intracorporeal anastomosis is performed between the terminal ileum and descending colon, with both enterotomies closed manually.
